topic.name: Cricket History and Players
topic.description: This cluster discusses various cricketers and significant cricket events in history, focusing on their careers and contributions to the sport.

England whitewashed Bangladesh in both the Test and ODI series
Document 2: Cameron White is an Australian cricketer for the Melbourne Renegades

Document 3: Lindsay Hassett was a cricketer who played for Victoria and Australia
He established himself with three consecutive first-class tons at the start of the campaign

He represented Kent County Cricket Club and is a former member of the England Test match and One Day International sides
Document 5: Stephen Newton was an English cricketer who represented and captained Somerset County Cricket Club
He made his debut in first-class cricket for Cambridge University
Document 6: Ray Lindwall is widely regarded as one of the greatest fast bowlers of all time
He represented Australia in 61 Tests from 1946 to 1960
Document 7: Ron Saggers was a member of Donald Bradman's famous Australian cricket team of 1948
This unprecedented feat by a Test side touring England earned them the sobriquet The Invincibles
Document 8: Doug Ring was an Australian cricketer who played for Victoria and Australia in 13 Tests
He topped the Victorian Cricket Association's second-grade bowling averages

topic.name: Roller Coaster Development
topic.description: The documents discuss the history, design, and features of various roller coasters around the world.

Document 1: Abyss is a steel roller coaster located at the Adventure World amusement park in Perth, Western Australia.
The Abyss is a Euro-Fighter, a roller coaster model from Gerstlauer that features a 'beyond-vertical' first drop.
Document 2: Goliath is a steel coaster roller coaster at the La Ronde located in Montreal, Quebec, Canada.
Designed by Bolliger & Mabillard, it reaches a maximum height of 174.8 feet.
Document 3: Kraken is a steel floorless roller coaster located at SeaWorld Orlando in the United States.
It features a total of seven inversions including two vertical loops.
Document 4: Dæmonen is a steel Floorless Coaster roller coaster at the Tivoli Gardens amusement park located in Copenhagen, Denmark.
Designed by Bolliger & Mabillard, it reaches a height of 92 feet.
Document 5: Wildfire is a steel roller coaster located at Silver Dollar City in Branson, Missouri.
The ride was built by Swiss firm Bolliger & Mabillard and opened in 2001.
Document 6: A Wing Coaster is a type of steel roller coaster manufactured by Bolliger & Mabillard where pairs of riders sit on either side of a roller coaster track.
Development of the Wing Coaster began between 2007 and 2008.
Document 7: Apocalypse is a steel stand-up roller coaster located at Six Flags America in Prince George's County, Maryland.
The ride made its debut in 1990 as Iron Wolf at Six Flags Great America.
Document 8: Superman: Escape from Krypton is a steel shuttle roller coaster located at Six Flags Magic Mountain in Valencia, California.
When it opened in 1997, it was the tallest roller coaster in the world.
Document 9: Green Lantern Coaster is a steel roller coaster at Warner Bros. Movie World on the Gold Coast, Queensland, Australia.
The ride is themed after DC Comics' Green Lantern.
Document 10: Storm Coaster is a Water Coaster located at the Sea World theme park on the Gold Coast, Australia.
The ride is designed by German firm Mack Rides and combines the flume and splashdown elements.

topic.name: Legal Rights and Regulations
topic.description: The documents discuss various legal frameworks and historical contexts surrounding individual rights, regulations, and judicial processes in the United States and Singapore.

Document 1: The legal history of cannabis in the United States relates to the regulation of cannabis use for medical, recreational or industrial purposes.
outright prohibitions began in the 1920s.
Document 2: The Fourteenth Amendment addresses citizenship rights and equal protection of the laws.
The amendment is one of the most litigated parts of the Constitution.
Document 3: Article 14 of the Constitution of the Republic of Singapore guarantees to Singapore citizens the rights to freedom of speech and expression.
the enjoyment of these rights may be restricted by laws imposed by the Parliament of Singapore.
Document 4: The Old Court – New Court controversy was a 19th-century political controversy in the U.S. state of Kentucky.

Document 5: The United States Constitution contains several provisions regarding the law of criminal procedure.
Several of these rights regulate pre-trial procedure.
Document 9: The American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) is a nonpartisan, non-profit organization whose stated mission is to defend and preserve the individual rights and liberties guaranteed to every person in this country by the Constitution and laws of the United States.

topic.name: Basketball History and Teams
topic.description: This topic encompasses the history, achievements, and notable events related to various basketball teams and players in professional and collegiate leagues.

Document 1: The Portland Trail Blazers, commonly known as the Blazers, are an American professional basketball team based in Portland, Oregon.
The franchise entered the league as an expansion team in 1970, and has enjoyed a strong following.
Document 2: Jonathan James 'Jon' Scheyer is an American former basketball player, currently an assistant coach of the Duke Blue Devils men's basketball team.
Scheyer led his high school team to an Illinois state basketball championship as a high school All-American.
Document 3: The 2011–12 Ivy League men's basketball season marked the continuation of the annual tradition of competitive basketball among Ivy League members.
Harvard was the preseason favorite for the first time and spent most of the season receiving votes in the 2011–12 national rankings.
Document 4: The 2012–13 Ivy League men's basketball season marked the continuation of the annual tradition of competitive basketball among Ivy League members.
Due to a cheating scandal that involved defending champion Harvard, Princeton was the preseason favorite.
Document 5: The 2015 NBL Canada Finals brawl was an altercation that occurred prior to Game 7 of the year's National Basketball League of Canada Finals.
It led to the Rainmen's forfeit of the deciding game and allowed the Express to win the championship by default.
Document 6: The 2012–13 Harvard Crimson men's basketball team represents Harvard University during the 2012–13 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The team entered the season as the two-time defending Ivy League Champion.
Document 7: The 2011–12 Harvard Crimson men's basketball team represented Harvard University in the Ivy League athletic conference during the 2011–12 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The team set a school record with 26 wins during the season and tied a school record for conference wins with 12.
Document 8: The 2009 NBA All-Star Game was an exhibition basketball game played on February 15, 2009 at US Airways Center in Phoenix, Arizona.
The game was the 58th edition of the National Basketball Association (NBA) All-Star Game.

topic.name: Animal Breeds and Their Characteristics
topic.description: This topic encompasses various animal breeds, focusing on their origins, characteristics, and uses in human society.

Document 1: The American Saddlebred is a horse breed from the United States.
Averaging 15 to 16 hands in height, Saddlebreds are known for their sense of presence and style.
Document 3: Highland cattle are a Scottish cattle breed.
They have long horns and long wavy coats that are coloured black, brindle, red, yellow, white, silver or dun.
Document 4: Kaimanawa horses are a population of feral horses in New Zealand.
They are known for their hardiness and quiet temperament.
Document 7: The Dales pony is one of the United Kingdom's native mountain and moorland pony breeds.
The breed is known for its strength, hardiness, stamina, courage, intelligence, and good disposition.
Document 8: The Arabian horse is a breed that originated on the Arabian Peninsula.
It is also one of the oldest breeds, with archaeological evidence of horses in the Middle East that resemble modern Arabians dating back 4,500 years.

topic.name: Major League Baseball Players
topic.description: This topic encompasses the careers and achievements of various professional baseball players in Major League Baseball (MLB) across different eras.

Document 1: Jimmy Lavender was an American professional baseball player who played in Major League Baseball as a pitcher from 1912 to 1917.
He played a total of five seasons with the Chicago Cubs of the National League from 1912 to 1916.
Document 2: Jack Stivetts was a professional baseball pitcher who played 11 seasons in Major League Baseball spanning from 1889 to 1899.
He played in the American Association with the St. Louis Browns, and in the National League with the Boston Beaneaters and Cleveland Spiders.
Document 3: Phil Hughes is an American right-handed professional baseball pitcher for the Minnesota Twins of Major League Baseball.
He played in MLB for the New York Yankees from 2007 through 2013.
Document 4: Sammy Vick was an American professional right fielder in Major League Baseball.
He played for the New York Yankees from 1917 to 1920, and the Boston Red Sox in 1921.
Document 5: Bill Stein is a retired professional baseball player and manager.
His playing career spanned 17 seasons, 14 of which were spent in Major League Baseball.
Document 6: Hack Wilson was an American Major League Baseball player who played 12 seasons for the New York Giants, Chicago Cubs, Brooklyn Dodgers and Philadelphia Phillies.
He was one of the most accomplished power hitters in the game during the late 1920s and early 1930s.
Document 7: Juan Uribe is a Dominican professional baseball third baseman for the Cleveland Indians of Major League Baseball.
He previously played in MLB for the Colorado Rockies, Chicago White Sox, San Francisco Giants, Los Angeles Dodgers, Atlanta Braves and New York Mets.
Document 8: Jeff Heath was a Canadian-born American left fielder in Major League Baseball who played most of his career for the Cleveland Indians.
He was one of the American League's most promising power hitters of the late 1930s and early 1940s.
Document 9: The 1962 National League tie-breaker series was a three-game playoff series at the conclusion of Major League Baseball's 1962 regular season to determine the winner of the National League pennant.
Document 10: Nap Lajoie was an American professional baseball second baseman and player-manager.
He played in Major League Baseball for the Philadelphia Phillies, Philadelphia Athletics, and Cleveland Naps between 1896 and 1916.

topic.name: Popular Songs and Artists
topic.description: This topic encompasses notable songs and artists in the pop and country music genres, highlighting their releases, themes, and critical reception.

1: Back to December is a song written and recorded by American singer/songwriter Taylor Swift for her third studio album Speak Now.
The song is considered an orchestral country pop ballad and its lyrics are a remorseful plea for forgiveness for breaking up with a former lover.
2: Sweat is a song recorded by American recording artist Ciara for her self-titled fifth album Ciara.
The song received mixed reviews from music critics, with some describing it as a heavy club song.
3: Gone Too Soon is a ballad recorded and popularized by American musician Michael Jackson.
Jackson's version of Gone Too Soon was dedicated to the memory of Jackson's friend Ryan White.
4: The Beyoncé Experience Live is a live DVD by American recording artist Beyoncé, released by Columbia Records.
The show featured guest appearances from rapper Jay-Z on 'Upgrade U' and former Destiny's Child mates Michelle Williams and Kelly Rowland.
5: The Edge of Glory is a song by American singer Lady Gaga from her second studio album, Born This Way.
The song received critical acclaim, with many reviewers deeming it as an album highlight.
6: Sometimes is a song recorded by American singer Britney Spears for her debut studio album, ... Baby One More Time.
The song received generally mixed reviews from contemporary critics.
7: Stupid Girl is a song recorded by alternative rock band Garbage for the band's self-titled debut studio album.
The song features lyrics about female empowerment.
8: The Singles Collection is the second greatest hits album of American singer Britney Spears.
The album was praised by contemporary critics, who noted Spears's impact and influence on pop music.
9: Breathe is a country pop song written and performed by American singer-songwriters Taylor Swift and Colbie Caillat.
The song received favorable responses from contemporary music critics.
10: I Got You is a song performed by British singer and songwriter Leona Lewis for her second studio album, Echo.
The song garnered a mixed response from music critics.

topic.name: Oslo Metro System
topic.description: The documents discuss various aspects of the Oslo Metro system, including its trains, stations, and infrastructure.

1: T1000 and T1300 were two rapid transit train classes used on Oslo Metro in Oslo, Norway.
They were the first metro trains used in Oslo, and had remained in active use until being replaced by OS MX3000 trains in 2007.
2: Nordberg was a station on the Sognsvann Line of the Oslo Metro in Oslo, Norway.
The station saw several accidents, and was closed on 5 May 1992 when the Sognsvann Line was upgraded to rapid transit standard.
4: MX3000 is an electric train used on Oslo Metro in Oslo, Norway.
They replaced the older T1000 and T1300 stock that was used on the Oslo Metro since 1966.
5: The Ring Line is the newest rapid transit line of the Oslo Metro of Oslo, Norway.
The 5.0 kilometres long line has three stations: Nydalen, Storo and Sinsen.

topic.name: Colonial American Politics
topic.description: The documents discuss key figures, events, and political dynamics in Colonial America leading up to and during the Revolutionary War.

Document 1: Thomas Hutchinson was a prominent Loyalist politician of the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
He was blamed for contributing to the tensions that led to the American Revolutionary War.
Document 2: The Pennsylvania Mutiny of 1783 was an anti-government protest by soldiers of the Continental Army.
The mutiny ultimately resulted in Congress vacating Philadelphia.
Document 3: The Middle Colonies were the most ethnically and religiously diverse British colonies.
Civil unrest in Europe and other colonies saw an influx of immigrants to the Middle Colonies.
Document 4: Richard Bellingham was a colonial magistrate and several-time governor of the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
He was one of the architects of the Massachusetts Body of Liberties.
Document 9: Isaac Parsons was an American planter, politician, and militia officer in Virginia.
He served as a captain in the Virginia militia during the American Revolutionary War.
Document 10: Robert White was a distinguished early American military officer, lawyer, judge, and politician in Virginia.
He served in the Virginia militia and Continental Army during the American Revolutionary War.

topic.name: Royal Australian Air Force Training and Operations
topic.description: The documents focus on various training units and operational commands of the Royal Australian Air Force during World War II.

1: RAF Coastal Command was a formation within the Royal Air Force (RAF)
Its primary task was to protect convoys from the German Kriegsmarine's U-boat force.
2: Central Flying School (CFS) is a Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) training unit
It is also home to the 'Roulettes' aerobatic team.
3: The Connecticut Wing Civil Air Patrol is the highest echelon of the Civil Air Patrol in the state of Connecticut
Members were notably instrumental in major events during the Wing's 66-year history.
4: No. 3 Elementary Flying Training School (No. 3 EFTS) was a Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) pilot training unit
It was one of twelve elementary flying training schools employed by the RAAF.
5: Eastern Area Command was one of several geographically based commands raised by the Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) during World War II
It was primarily responsible for air defence, aerial reconnaissance and protection of the sea lanes.
6: No. 482 Squadron was a maintenance unit of the Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF)
It was formed in May 1942 as No. 4 Repair and Salvage Unit.
9: No. 486 Squadron was a maintenance unit of the Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF)
It was formed in August 1946 as part of No. 86 (Transport) Wing.
10: Squadron Leader James Archibald Findlay MacLachlan DSO, DFC & Two Bars was a Royal Air Force (RAF) fighter pilot
MacLachlan was credited with 16 German and Italian aircraft shot down in approximately 250 missions.

topic.name: Naval Warfare History
topic.description: The documents discuss various naval ships, their designs, roles, and histories, particularly in the context of early 20th-century conflicts.

Document 1: Gangut-class battleships were the first dreadnoughts for the Imperial Russian Navy before World War I.
Their role was to defend the mouth of the Gulf of Finland against the Germans.
Document 2: HMS Daring was a D-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy in the early 1930s.
Daring escorted convoys in the Red Sea in October – November 1939.
Document 3: Re Umberto was a Re Umberto-class ironclad battleship built for the Italian Regia Marina in the 1880s.
She saw limited action during the Italo-Turkish War in 1911 – 12.
Document 4: Lützow was a heavy cruiser of Nazi Germany's Kriegsmarine, the fifth and final member of the Admiral Hipper class.
The vessel was still incomplete when sold to the Soviet Union.
Document 5: The Evstafi class were a pair of pre-dreadnought battleships of the Imperial Russian Navy built before World War I.
Both ships covered several bombardments of the Bosphorus fortifications in early 1915.
Document 6: The Emanuele Filiberto was a pre-dreadnought battleship built for the Italian Navy during the 1890s.
She was involved in the assaults on Tripoli in North Africa during the Italo-Turkish War.
Document 7: Admiral Makarov was the second of the four Bayan-class armoured cruisers built for the Imperial Russian Navy.
She fought several inconclusive battles with German ships during the war.
Document 8: Nisshin was a Kasuga-class armored cruiser of the Imperial Japanese Navy.
During the Russo-Japanese War of 1904 – 05, Nisshin participated in the Battle of the Yellow Sea.
Document 9: HMS Diana was a D-class destroyer of the Royal Navy.
She served with the Home Fleet during the Norwegian Campaign.
Document 10: Napoli was a Regina Elena-class pre-dreadnought battleship built for the Italian Regia Marina.
She took part in the attack on Derna, Libya, during the Italo-Turkish War.

topic.name: Historic Architecture and Landmarks
topic.description: This cluster focuses on significant historical buildings and structures, their architectural styles, and their cultural importance across various locations.

Document 1: Bridgeport Harbor Light was a lighthouse in Bridgeport, Connecticut, originally constructed in 1851.
The lighthouse caught fire and was destroyed during the dismantling in 1953.
Document 2: St Mary's Church is an Anglican parish church in the village of Newbold Astbury, Cheshire, England.
The church's architecture has been praised by a number of writers.
Document 3: The Zodiac settle is a piece of painted furniture designed by the English architect and designer William Burges.
Burges desired to fill his home with furniture 'covered with paintings, both ornaments and subjects.'
Document 4: The Scarborough Historic District was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1984.
The district contains seven historically and architecturally significant properties dating from the late 18th century to the early 20th century.
Document 5: Citibase Brighton is a complex of serviced offices on a prominent elevated position in the Round Hill area of Brighton.
The building has had three greatly different uses since its construction at the edge of Brighton parish in 1854.
Document 6: Ambler's Texaco Gas Station is a historic filling station located at the intersection of Old U.S. Route 66 and Illinois Route 17.
It was added to the U.S. National Register of Historic Places in 2001.
Document 7: The Groton Monument is dedicated to the defenders who fell during the Battle of Groton Heights on September 6, 1781.
The monument was originally 127 feet high, but it was later changed in 1881.
Document 8: Loughor Castle is a ruined, medieval fortification located in the town of Loughor, Wales.
The castle was built around 1106 by the Anglo-Norman lord Henry de Beaumont.
Document 9: The architecture of Scotland in the Middle Ages includes all building within the modern borders of Scotland.
Medieval parish church architecture was typically simpler than in England.
Document 10: Wressle Castle is a ruined palace-fortress in the East Riding of Yorkshire, England, built for Thomas Percy in the 1390s.
The castle was intended as a high-status residence rather than a fortress.

topic.name: Historical Battles and Conflicts
topic.description: This cluster discusses various significant battles and military engagements throughout history, highlighting the tactics, outcomes, and historical contexts of each conflict.

Document 1: Spanish conquest of the Maya was a protracted conflict during the Spanish colonisation of the Americas
the conquest began in the early 16th century and is generally considered to have ended in 1697.
Document 2: The Battle of Artemisium was a series of naval engagements over three days during the second Persian invasion of Greece
the battle took place simultaneously with the more famous land battle at Thermopylae.
Document 3: The Battle of Bunker Hill was fought on June 17, 1775, during the Siege of Boston
the battle is named after Bunker Hill in Charlestown, Massachusetts.
Document 4: The Battle of Great Bridge was fought December 9, 1775, in the area of Great Bridge, Virginia
the victory by Continental Army and militia forces led to the departure of Governor Lord Dunmore.
Document 5: The Battle of Pegae was fought between 11 and 18 March 921 in the outskirts of Constantinople
the battle took place in a locality called Pegae.
Document 6: The Battle of Hama was fought some 24 km from the city of Hama in Syria on 29 – 30 November 903
the Abbasids were victorious, resulting in the capture and execution of the Qarmatian leadership.
Document 7: The Siege of Pensacola was two separate attempts in 1707 by English-supported Creek Indians to capture the town and fortress of Pensacola
the first siege resulted in the destruction of the town.
Document 8: Triarii were one of the elements of the early Roman military Manipular legions
they were the oldest and among the wealthiest men in the army.
Document 9: The Battle of Solachon was fought in 586 CE in northern Mesopotamia between the East Roman forces and the Sassanid Persians
the engagement was part of the long and inconclusive Byzantine – Sassanid War.
Document 10: Justin was an East Roman aristocrat and general
he fought against the Slavs, the Sassanid Persians and supervised the Byzantine Empire's first contacts with the Avars.

expected to produce about 50 megawatts of electricity

topic.name: World War II Fighter Aircraft
topic.description: The documents discuss various fighter aircraft developed and used during World War II, highlighting their design, development, and operational history.

Document 1: The Lavochkin La-7 was a piston-engined Soviet fighter developed during World War II
It was felt by its pilots to be at least the equal of any German piston-engined fighter.
Document 2: The Supermarine Spitfire is a British single-seat fighter aircraft used by the Royal Air Force during the Second World War
The Spitfire was designed as a short-range, high-performance interceptor aircraft.
Document 3: The UK operated the McDonnell Douglas F-4 Phantom II as one of its principal combat aircraft from the 1960s to the early 1990s
The Phantom was procured to serve in both the Fleet Air Arm and Royal Air Force.
Document 4: The Arado E.381 was a proposed parasite fighter aircraft for Germany's Luftwaffe during World War II
Development was cancelled due to lack of funds and official support.
Document 5: The General Dynamics F-16 Fighting Falcon is a single-engine multirole fighter aircraft originally developed for the United States Air Force.
Designed as an air superiority day fighter, it evolved into a successful all-weather multirole aircraft.
Document 6: The North American XB-21 was a prototype bomber aircraft developed for evaluation by the United States Army Air Corps.
It was found to be considerably more expensive than the rival aircraft.
Document 7: The Hall XPTBH was a prototype American twin-engined seaplane submitted to the United States Navy in response to a 1934 specification.
The XPTBH proved successful in flight testing.
Document 8: The Boeing B-52 Stratofortress is a long-range, subsonic, jet-powered strategic bomber.
The B-52 was designed and built by Boeing, which has continued to provide support and upgrades.
Document 9: The Lockheed AH-56 Cheyenne was an attack helicopter developed for the United States Army.
The AH-56's maiden flight took place on 21 September 1967.
Document 10: The Interstate TDR was an early unmanned combat aerial vehicle developed during the Second World War.
It saw some service in the Pacific Theater against the Japanese.

topic.name: Medieval European Rulers
topic.description: The documents discuss various rulers and their political dynamics in medieval Europe, particularly focusing on Hungary and the Byzantine Empire.

1: Solomon, also Salomon (Hungarian: Salamon; 1053 – 1087) was King of Hungary from 1063.
He officially abdicated in 1081, but was arrested for conspiring against Géza's brother and successor, Ladislaus.
7: Géza I (Hungarian pronunciation: [ˈɡeːzɒ]; Hungarian: I. Géza; c. 1040 – 25 April 1077) was King of Hungary from 1074 until his death.
Géza closely cooperated with Solomon, but their relationship became tense from 1071.
9: Coloman the Learned, also the Book-Lover or the Bookish (Hungarian: Könyves Kálmán; c. 1070 – 3 February 1116) was King of Hungary from 1095 and King of Croatia from 1097 until his death.
Coloman was crowned in early 1096; the circumstances of his accession to the throne are unknown.

topic.name: Diverse Fungal Species
topic.description: This cluster discusses various species of fungi, their characteristics, taxonomy, and ecological associations.

Document 1: Lactarius alnicola, commonly known as the golden milkcap, is a species of fungus in the Russulaceae family.
The fungus is found in the western United States and Mexico, where it grows in mycorrhizal associations with various coniferous trees.
Document 2: Podoserpula is a genus of fungi in the family Amylocorticiaceae.
The genus is known to occur in Australia and New Zealand, Venezuela, Madagascar, and New Caledonia.
Document 3: Strobilurus tenacellus, commonly known as the pinecone cap, is a species of agaric fungus in the family Physalacriaceae.
It is found in Asia and Europe, where it grows on the fallen cones of pine and spruce trees.
Document 4: Mycena arcangeliana is a species of Mycenaceae fungus.
The species grows on dead wood in autumn months, and can be found throughout Europe.
Document 5: Russula densifolia, commonly known as the crowded russula or the reddening russula, is a species of agaric fungus in the family Russulaceae.
A widespread species, it is found in Asia, Europe, and North America.
Document 6: Mycena atkinsoniana is a species of agaric fungus in the Mycenaceae family.
The species was first described in 1935 as Mycena fagicola.
Document 7: Suillus americanus is a species of fungus in the Suillaceae family of mushrooms.
It grows in a mycorrhizal association with eastern white pine.
Document 8: Amanita regalis, commonly known as the royal fly agaric, is a species of fungus in the Amanitaceae family.
Common in Scandinavian countries, it is also found in eastern and northern Europe.
Document 9: Psilocybe makarorae is a species of psilocybin mushroom in the family Strophariaceae.
It is known only from New Zealand, where it grows on rotting wood.
Document 10: Amanita ravenelii, commonly known as the pinecone Lepidella, is a species of fungus in the family Amanitaceae.
The fruit bodies are medium to large, with caps up to 17 cm wide.

topic.name: Paralympic Sports and Athletes
topic.description: This topic encompasses the participation, achievements, and experiences of athletes with disabilities in the Paralympic Games.

1: Great Britain competed at the 2010 Winter Paralympics held in Vancouver, Canada.

3: John McFall is a Cardiff-based British Paralympic sprinter.

8: Edina Müller is a German 2.5 point wheelchair basketball player and KL1 canoeist.

10: Bernadette Peters is an American actress, singer and children's book author.

topic.name: Film and Television Industry

7: Kirk Cameron is best known for his role as Mike Seaver on the ABC sitcom Growing Pains.

10: Resident Evil: Afterlife is a 2010 3D science fiction action horror film directed by Paul W. S. Anderson.
The film follows Alice searching for and rescuing the remaining survivors in Los Angeles.

topic.name: Occultism and Spiritual Movements
topic.description: This topic encompasses the exploration of various occult and spiritual movements, including their historical figures, philosophical underpinnings, and cultural impacts.

10: The New Age is a term applied to a range of spiritual or religious beliefs and practices that developed in Western nations during the 1970s.

topic.name: Notable American Figures
topic.description: This cluster discusses notable American figures from various fields, including politics, education, military service, and the arts.

1: Eisenhower National Historic Site preserves the home and farm of Dwight D. Eisenhower, the 34th President of the United States.
It was also a successful cattle operation, with a show herd of black Angus cattle.
2: Howard Hille Johnson was a blind American educator and writer in the states of Virginia and West Virginia.

3: Casey Donovan was an American male pornographic actor of the 1970s and 1980s.
He appeared in the film that would cement his status as a gay icon, Boys in the Sand, in 1971.
4: Moses Hardy was, at age 112, the last surviving black veteran of World War I.
He served in the segregated 805th infantry, which was assigned a variety of manual labor and support tasks.
5: Yamashita Yoshitsugu was the first person to have been awarded 10th degree red belt in Kodokan judo.
He was also a pioneer of judo in the United States.

7: Paul Conrad was an American political cartoonist and winner of three Pulitzer Prizes for editorial cartooning.

9: Shangguan Yunzhu was a Chinese actress active from the 1940s to the 1960s.
She was considered one of the most talented and versatile actresses in China.
10: William Ford Robinson Stanley was a British inventor with 78 patents filed in both the United Kingdom and the United States of America.
He was a noted philanthropist, who gave over £80,000 to education projects during the last 15 years of his life.

topic.name: Naval Warfare History
topic.description: The documents collectively discuss various naval engagements, campaigns, and notable figures within the context of historical maritime conflicts.

The submarine conducted seven patrols and sank 38 ships during the war.
2: Wat Tyler Cluverius, Jr. was an admiral in the United States Navy.

3: The Sunda Strait campaign was a series of naval actions between French and British forces.

The incident was part of the East Indies campaign of the French Revolutionary Wars.
5: The Meermin slave mutiny took place on a Dutch East India Company slave ship.

6: Ben Carlin was an adventurer who circumnavigated the world in an amphibious vehicle.

7: The Battle of the Îles Saint-Marcouf was fought during the French Revolutionary Wars.

8: Sir Nathaniel Dance was an officer of the Honourable East India Company.

9: The SS Gothenburg was a steamship that wrecked on the Great Barrier Reef.
The ship's wreck involved significant loss of life.
10: The Battle of Orbetello was a naval engagement of the Franco-Spanish War.
It was fought between French and Spanish fleets off the coast of Tuscany.

topic.name: The Boat Race History
topic.description: A historical overview of the annual rowing competition between the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge.

Document 1: The Boat Race is an annual side-by-side rowing race between crews from the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge along the River Thames.
First held in 1829, the race takes place on the 4.2 miles Championship Course on the River Thames in southwest London.
Document 3: Held annually, the Boat Race is a side-by-side rowing race between crews from the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge along the River Thames.

topic.name: State Highways and Parkways
topic.description: The documents discuss various state highways and parkways in the United States and Canada, detailing their routes, history, and significance.

Document 1: Adams Avenue Parkway is a one-half-mile-long private toll road in Utah.
The roadway opened in 2001 at a cost of $8.9 million.
Document 2: Mississippi Highway 444 is a short highway in western Mississippi.
MS 444 was designated in 1958, and became paved two years later.
Document 3: Delaware Route 8 is a state highway located in Kent County in Delaware.
The road was built as a state highway west of Dover by 1924.
Document 4: Ontario Highway 90 was a provincially maintained highway in Ontario.
The highway was designated in 1937.
Document 5: The Majura Parkway is an 11.5-kilometre-long parkway under construction in the ACT.
The parkway has been in planning since the 1970s.
Document 6: Maryland Route 2 is the longest state highway in Maryland.
The route runs from Solomons Island in Calvert County north to Baltimore.
Document 7: M-179 is a 16.963-mile-long state trunkline highway in Michigan.
The highway crosses through forest land and provides access to state recreational areas.
Document 8: Highway 127 is a provincially maintained highway in Ontario.
The route begins in Maynooth, connecting Highway 62 with Highway 60.
Document 9: M-67 is a north-south state trunkline highway in Michigan.
The highway has largely been unchanged during its history.
Document 10: Maryland Route 537 is a collection of unsigned state highways in Maryland.
These highways are segments of old alignment of U.S. Route 213.

topic.name: Marine and Freshwater Species
topic.description: The documents discuss various species of marine and freshwater animals, including sharks, fish, and unique aquatic creatures.

1: The silvertip shark is a large species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae.
This species is often encountered around offshore islands and coral reefs.
2: Toxotes chatareus is a species of perciform fish in the archerfish genus Toxotes.
They are omnivorous, feeding on insects, fish, and vegetative matter at the surface of the water.
3: The whiskery shark is a species of houndshark, belonging to the family Triakidae.
It prefers rocky and vegetated habitats.
5: The common torpedo is a species of electric ray in the family Torpedinidae, found in the Mediterranean Sea and the eastern Atlantic Ocean.
It can deliver a strong electric shock of up to 200 volts.
6: Alligator gar are ray-finned euryhaline fish related to bowfin in the infraclass Holostei.
They are among the largest freshwater fishes in North America.
7: The velvet belly lanternshark is a species of dogfish shark in the family Etmopteridae.
The velvet belly is bioluminescent, with light-emitting photophores.
9: The crocodile shark is a species of mackerel shark and the only extant member of the family Pseudocarchariidae.
It is an active-swimming predator of pelagic bony fishes, squid, and shrimp.
10: The blacktip shark is a species of requiem shark, and part of the family Carcharhinidae.
It is common to coastal tropical and subtropical waters around the world.

topic.name: Insect and Animal Biology
topic.description: The documents discuss various species of insects and animals, their characteristics, behaviors, and ecological roles.

1: The meat ant is a species of ant endemic to Australia.
It is characterised by its dark-bluish body and red head.
2: Polistes exclamans is a social wasp and is part of the family Vespidae.
P. exclamans has three specific castes, including males, workers, and queens.
7: Spiders are air-breathing arthropods that have eight legs and chelicerae with fangs that inject venom.
Spiders are found worldwide on every continent except for Antarctica.
10: Entoprocta is a phylum of mostly sessile aquatic animals.
Mature individuals are goblet-shaped, on relatively long stalks.

topic.name: World War II Military Units
topic.description: This topic encompasses various military units and their roles during World War II, highlighting their formations, operations, and notable engagements.

1: John Basilone was a United States Marine Corps Gunnery Sergeant who received the nation's highest military award for valor, the Medal of Honor, for heroism during the Battle of Guadalcanal in World War II.
He was killed in action on the first day of the Battle of Iwo Jima.
2: The 3rd Sustainment Brigade has served three tours in Iraq in support of Operation Iraqi Freedom, its first tour being the initial invasion and securing of the country.
The unit was not designated as a separate unit until 2005.
3: The 2/18th Battalion was an Australian Army infantry unit that served during World War II.
The battalion participated in the unsuccessful defence of Singapore in early February 1942.
4: 2nd Battalion, 9th Marines played an instrumental role in the defeat of the Japanese forces in the Battles of Guam and Iwo Jima during World War II.
The battalion distinguished itself in the defense of Khe Sanh during the Vietnam War.
5: The 174th Infantry Brigade has a long and decorated history when designated as the supply command of the 1st Cavalry Division, having seen service in every major conflict of the 20th century in support of its parent division.
The brigade was deployed for both World War I and World War II.
6: The 10th Combat Aviation Brigade supported the division as it undertook numerous operations and overseas contingencies in the 1990s, including Operation Restore Hope, Operation Uphold Democracy, and Task Force Eagle, as well as disaster relief following Hurricane Andrew.
The brigade has since become involved in the War on Terrorism, seeing four deployments to Afghanistan to support Operation Enduring Freedom.
7: The 15th Sustainment Brigade has a long and decorated history when designated as the supply command of the 1st Cavalry Division, having seen service in every major conflict of the 20th century.
The brigade's lineage dates back to 1919.
8: The 373rd (Croatian) Infantry Division was a division of the German Army during World War II.
It was formed in June 1943 using a brigade from the Home Guard of the Independent State of Croatia.
9: The 42nd Military Police Brigade has a long history with United States Army Europe, having first activated as a customs unit in post-World War II Germany.
The brigade has seen several tours in support of the Iraq War's Operation Iraqi Freedom and Operation Enduring Freedom.
10: The 36th Engineer Brigade saw action in the North African Campaign and the Italian Campaign, and it eventually participated in the invasion of mainland Europe.
It has seen tours of duty in both Iraq and Afghanistan.

topic.name: Classic Video Games
topic.description: The documents discuss various classic video games across different genres and platforms, highlighting their gameplay mechanics, storylines, and reception.

Document 1: Ratchet & Clank is a 2002 3D platform video game developed by Insomniac Games

Document 9: F-Zero is a futuristic racing video game developed by Nintendo EAD

Document 10: Final Fantasy IV is a role-playing video game developed and published by Square
The game's story follows Cecil, a dark knight, as he tries to prevent the sorcerer Golbez from seizing powerful crystals.

topic.name: Comics and Fantasy Literature
topic.description: This cluster encompasses various forms of comics and fantasy literature, including graphic novels, pulp magazines, and manga, showcasing their unique narratives and cultural impact.

Document 7: The Witch's Tales was a pulp magazine edited by Tom Chadburn which published two issues in November and December 1936.

Document 8: Ghost Stories was a pulp magazine which published 64 issues between 1926 and 1932.

Document 9: Gin Tama is a Japanese manga written and illustrated by Hideaki Sorachi and serialized in Shueisha's Weekly Shōnen Jump.

Document 10: The Encyclopedia of Chicago is an historical reference work covering Chicago and the entire Chicago metropolitan area published by the University of Chicago Press.
It exists in both a hardcover print edition and an online format, known as the Electronic Encyclopedia of Chicago.

topic.name: Scientific Concepts and Applications
topic.description: This cluster discusses various scientific concepts and their applications, including elements from the periodic table, atmospheric phenomena, nuclear waste management, and medical procedures.

Document 1: Group 4 is a group of elements in the periodic table.
The three Group 4 elements that occur naturally are titanium (Ti), zirconium (Zr) and hafnium (Hf).
Document 2: A cold-core low, also known as an upper level low or cold-core cyclone, is a cyclone aloft which has an associated cold pool of air.

Document 3: The Yucca Mountain Nuclear Waste Repository was to be a deep geological repository storage facility for spent nuclear fuel.
The project has had many difficulties and was highly contested by the general public and many politicians.
Document 5: Tracheal intubation is the placement of a flexible plastic tube into the trachea to maintain an open airway.
It is frequently performed in critically injured, ill, or anesthetized patients to facilitate ventilation of the lungs.
Document 10: The actinide series encompasses the 15 metallic chemical elements with atomic numbers from 89 to 103.
All actinides are radioactive and release energy upon radioactive decay.

topic.name: Historical Figures and Nationalism
topic.description: The documents discuss various historical figures who played significant roles in nationalist movements and political changes across Europe and the Middle East during the 19th and 20th centuries.

1: Ernst Moritz Hess was a baptized German Jew who served in the Imperial German Army during the First World War.
He was classified as a 'full-blooded Jew' due to his mother being Jewish, even though he was baptised a Protestant.
2: Nikolai Ryzhkov is a former Soviet official who became a Russian politician following the dissolution of the Soviet Union.
Responsible for the cultural and economic administration of the Soviet Union during the late Gorbachev Era.
3: The German Eastern Marches Society was a radical, extremely nationalist xenophobic organization founded in 1894.
Its main aims were the promotion of Germanization of Poles living in Prussia and destruction of Polish national identity.
4: Erhard Heiden was an early member of the Nazi Party and the third commander of the paramilitary wing of Schutzstaffel (SS).
He was dismissed from his post in 1929 due to a drop in SS membership.
5: Izz ad-Din al-Qassam was a Syrian Muslim preacher and a leader in the local struggles against British and French Mandatory rule in the Levant.
He became a militant opponent of Zionism in the 1920s and 1930s.
6: Jezdimir Dangić was a Bosnian Serb Chetnik commander during World War II.
He commanded the gendarmerie unit that escorted King Peter II out of the country.
7: The Polish – Russian War of 1792 was fought between the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Russian Empire.
The war ended when the Polish King Stanisław August Poniatowski decided to seek a diplomatic solution.
8: Sultan Sir Abu Bakar Ibni Al-Marhum Tun Temenggung Raja Daing Ibrahim was known as 'The Father of Modern Johor.'
He initiated policies and provided aids to ethnic Chinese entrepreneurs to stimulate the development of the state.
9: Aqil Agha was the strongman of northern Palestine in the mid-19th century during Ottoman rule.
He was known for his protection of the local Christian and Jewish communities in Galilee.
10: The 2006 Riga summit discussed the War in Afghanistan and the future role and borders of NATO.
This summit was the first NATO summit held on territory of a former USSR republic.

topic.name: Monster-of-the-Week Episodes
topic.description: The documents discuss standalone episodes from the science fiction series The X-Files and Fringe that feature unique paranormal cases investigated by the main characters.

Document 1: 'Detour' is a 'Monster-of-the-Week' story, a stand-alone plot which is unconnected to the series' wider mythology.
The episode centers on FBI special agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully who work on cases linked to the paranormal.
Document 3: 'Improbable' is a 'monster-of-the-week' episode, a stand-alone plot which is unconnected to the mythology.
The episode centers on FBI special agents who work on cases linked to the paranormal, called X-Files.
Document 4: 'Hellbound' is a 'monster-of-the-week' episode, a stand-alone plot which is unconnected to the series' wider mythology.
The episode centers on FBI special agents who work on cases linked to the paranormal, called X-Files.
Document 7: 'Immortality' is an episode of the science fiction drama television series Fringe, involving a unique case investigated by the Fringe Division.
The episode features a plot centered around a series of deaths caused by flesh-eating 'skelter beetles'.
Document 8: 'Sanguinarium' is a 'Monster-of-the-Week' story, a stand-alone plot which is unconnected to the series' wider mythology.
The episode centers on bizarre murders in a hospital's plastic surgery unit leading to a supernatural investigation.

topic.name: Atlantic Hurricane Impacts
topic.description: This topic encompasses the development, intensification, and impacts of various hurricanes in the Atlantic basin, including their effects on land, damage caused, and the meteorological history associated with each storm.

Document 1: Hurricane Richard was a damaging hurricane that affected areas of Central America in October 2010
Hurricane Richard caused an estimated $ 80 million ( 2010 USD ) in damages in its path, much of which was in Belize.
Document 2: Hurricane Erick brought minor impact to the western coastline of Mexico in July 2013
In preparation for the cyclone, numerous tropical cyclone warnings and watches were issued for various portions of the coastline of Mexico.
Document 3: Hurricane Carol in 1953 was the strongest storm of the 1953 Atlantic hurricane season
At least 40 people required rescue, and four people were killed.
Document 4: Hurricane Easy was the fifth tropical storm, hurricane, and major hurricane of the 1950 Atlantic hurricane season
Hurricane Easy produced severe rainfall and strong waves, resulting in heavy damage in the Cedar Key area.
Document 5: Hurricane Betsy in 1956 was the first North Atlantic hurricane to make landfall in Puerto Rico in 24 years
The hurricane left $ 40 million in damage and 16 deaths, which prompted a federally declared disaster area.
Document 6: The 1989 Atlantic hurricane season featured the costliest tropical cyclone in the Atlantic basin at the time, Hurricane Hugo
Hurricane Hugo ranked as the costliest Atlantic hurricane until Hurricane Andrew in 1992.
Document 7: Hurricane Debby caused minor damage in the Greater and Lesser Antilles in August 2000
The heavy precipitation resulted in mudslides.
Document 8: Tropical Storm Ana was the only tropical cyclone on record in the North Atlantic basin to exist in the month of April
The storm brushed Bermuda with light rain.
Document 9: Hurricane Felix caused severe beach erosion along the East Coast of the United States in August 1995
Large waves in Puerto Rico caused minor coastal flooding in Cataño.
Document 10: Hurricane Hernan was the second of three Category 5 hurricanes during the 2002 Pacific hurricane season
Although Hernan remained far from land, swells of 15 to 20 feet caused minor beach erosion along the coast of Mexico.

topic.name: Higher Education and Community Development
topic.description: This topic encompasses the role of higher education institutions and community initiatives in shaping urban development and cultural identity in various cities across the United States.

Document 1: Norman is home to the University of Oklahoma, the largest university in the state, with approximately 30,000 students enrolled.
Economically the city has prominent higher education and related research industries.
Document 2: Greek organizations at Dartmouth provide both social and residential opportunities for students.
The first social fraternities were founded in 1842 and rapidly expanded to include the active participation of over half of the student body.
Document 5: the district has since been preserved and restored to appear much as it was during that period.
Many of them reflect the legacy of whaling in the city 's development.
